Hey, welcome to Magnetic Marketing Session number two, your first step to success, knowing who your ideal customer, client, or patient really is and how to reach them. Most business owners want to dive right into starting to create their marketing materials, but Dan says that is the wrong way to do it. They need to first define your target market. So that's what we're going to be talking about during this segment. But you know, people have had such great results using Magnetic Marketing and for people who own it already, if they're wondering, there's only people who get it and they wonder, does this really going to work? Does this really going to work for me? Well, I know it works. So Patrick Heller says, I'm a screen printer, a printer, customized t-shirts. And I went from 100K to 312K in one year with magnetic marketing. All right, let's talk about, this is, this is super important. A lot of people want to skip over this step to get right to creating the marketing. So the first thing is, why shouldn't we do that? Why shouldn't we go bang, just start creating, start creating, right? Well, because you are, you are essentially building something that may very well not match the person that is going to see it or the person you should choose to see it. And so when we leap to message, we're basically saying, here's what I got in my cart to sell. Here's my perception of it. And now I'm going to tell people about that. Not, gee, what, what will interest people and how can I make what I have in my cart match up to those interests? So it is really sort of a backwards process in many cases. And it can even, you know, it can be dangerous because the, the chasm between who's getting the messages and what their interests and motivations and life experiences and tribal language and all that is and what you put together, that chasm can be so great that literally you have little or no effectiveness. Right. And the other thing that people do, and this is key, is then they'll say, well, this magnetic marketing, this direct response marketing doesn't work, right? Where they've missed the fundamental. Yeah, they missed the fundamental. They missed the fundamental piece. Yeah. Yeah. You big broad messages, sort of the umbrella under which everybody can walk. Again, you see big companies do it, and you may be tempted to copy it, but their economics and agendas are very, very different than what most small businesses, startups, entrepreneurs and professionals are trying to do. So targeting matters more to us, right? And targeting is more and more possible and practical at the small business level. So it's kind of dumb not to do it. I mean, so if you were going to go out and knock on doors, and you were going to sell water purifiers, you could knock on every door. But what if you could know ahead of time that there was one house on that street that had harder water than all the rest of the water, right? And tasted bad, and they were constantly having to get plumbers out there to drain their pipes, right? And they had some sort of whiz bang, pure bread dog for which water sensitivity was, you know, an issue. And last week, they were down at Home Depot looking at water purifiers, but didn't buy anything. Would you want to go to that door? Or would you want to hope you hit it by random? Would you want to waste your time at all the doors? So that really is what targeting is all about. So what are some of the biggest mistakes that people make when they're getting into the selecting a target market? First of all, not selecting a target market. Yeah, well, of course. Yeah, I mean, your mistakes are in not really thinking through the profile of the person that you want to get in front of, and who is best matched with what you are going to be able to present. Because literally hundreds of thousands of these are sold. That's not a joke. And you're in every one of them. You're in every one of them. You're in every one of them. But there's two pages in the book, I always show everybody, that are like shocking to many because one is the profile of the person they have built who is the perfect prospective parishioner for his church. He makes the point, might be different for your church. Right. But here's our perfect profile, which they call Saddleback Sam because the church is named Saddleback. And so here's Sam and here's these 23, 24 points about Sam. And then the other page is the list of susceptibility, which is about timing. When are people most... And second of all, it's more predatory than, again, people would expect from a church. Right. So like items on it include they're in the middle of severe family problems, not moderate family, severe family problems with 18 who is getting in trouble with the law. Right. Right. Now... Getting divorced, someone who's just recently been divorced. Yeah. Now the one I just named, by the way, see Privacy's Dead, that information is readily available if you want to target market to that. Right. Okay. Which is exactly what he's suggesting. And you can clearly match a message to it. And people are sort of shocked to hear all of that coming from the church world. Let me address that though, because I think a lot of people have a problem with that. But what he would say, and I believe he would believe it, and I think it's true, he's going to help them. Well, of course, he's going to help them. You get to be most helpful to the people, you can be most helpful too. Right. So regardless of what you do, if you sell really nice ties, and it will make that person's life better, and he'll feel better about himself, and maybe he'll get a better job, or maybe he'll close a sale, or maybe he'll get a woman, or you know, whatever, because he's dressing better, you can't help him if you can't get him in the store and convince him to buy and wear a better tie. Right. So if we identify who's very susceptible, three months after a divorce, been married for more than 20 years, etc. Okay, he hasn't had to worry about what he looks like in 10. He kind of figured she wasn't going anywhere, and he was wrong. And now he's back in the game. He's very susceptible to we need to bring your wardrobe up to stuff, right. So the ability to do business with somebody, and then presuming that whatever you're doing when you do business is useful to that somebody, and beneficial to that somebody, is heightened by target marketing. Right. Well, the beauty of the beauty of magnetic marketing too is you don't have to do business with people who you really can't help, who you really can't serve. You don't have to. You can select who you want to do business with. Well, yeah, that's how you run your whole business life. Most business people feel like, well, first of all, they all start out, almost all start from a position of both desperation and ignorance, which is an ugly combination, which basically says anybody with a pulse in a while. And that does two bad things. It destroys differentiation, causes commoditization, price suppression, all of that. And secondly, it sticks you with a bunch of customers, clients or patients you report match with, and you don't like, and they don't like you when you're a friend of mine, Dennis, years ago, he had signed behind the door in his office that said to remind him every time you can stand anybody for 30 minutes. But the smart person says, why put myself in a position where I have to stand somebody for 30 minutes? Why not create a business where actually I like everybody I'm doing business with? Well, the answer to that question, most people would give you is you can't. Well, yes, you can. You sure can. And if you've got enough lead flow, deal flow coming in, then you can pick and choose who you're going to work with, who you're not going to work with, your price goes up, everything gets better in your business with magnetic marketing. Now, in the toolkit that you own, if you own it, in the resources section, you're going to see this, can we get this up there? Can we get a close up of that? Okay. All right. That's good enough. Okay. So, well, you can cut it in the video. Yeah, we'll cut it. Or I could get up out of the chair and really walk up there. That could be ugly on many, many levels. But this is, this is talking about defining, we really go into great depth here on how to define your target market. And we actually do it then on three levels, the demographic information. So let's start with what exactly is that? That's, I think that's where most people start and stop. Yeah, it's where most people start and stop. It's, in most cases, it's, it's the best thinking anybody knows there is to do. It falls in, there's 17 criteria for, for how you pick a market and pick a segment of a market and only a few of those things are really demographic. So demographic is your basic stats, right? Age, gender, maybe household income, maybe household net worth, married, not married, have kids, don't have kids, have pets, don't have pets, own car, don't own car, age of car owned, that kind of statistical information. And when you stop there, what you don't get to, you never break through into motivations, interests, desires, you only know statistical facts. So example, I always use it and now it's old news, but there was a sea change in the law a decade ago, I think, and most states that previously kept DMV, motor vehicle information confidential, began to sell it and rent it. And briefly, everybody in the weight loss industry had an orgasm because they thought, oh boy, right, for every one of our weight loss centers in, in a 50 mile radius, we can get a list of short fat, dumpy women, right? Because you can get height, you can get weight, right? And we can get them any age we want. And so we can get one that fits who's coming through our doors, right? demographic, it's demographics, right? It does not tell us if she cares, right? Or if she cares enough to do what it is that we sell, right? So one level of caring by a pill, swallow a pill, whole another level of caring come to gym, right? Whole another level of caring come to gym three times a week, if that's what we need her to do in order to make our business work. So everybody ran to these demographic lists and found them woefully unproductive, because all it gave us was the demographic profile, not the psychological profile, not the behavioral profile, not the, the buying pattern, right, profile. And so sometimes it's enough, right? What cases would it be enough? Well, many businesses and the broader your business, the more likely, right? So if you're running Walmart, chances are all you need is income, age, and they got kids. And you can probably profitably send packages of coupons and get a Walmart credit card and get a free 12 pack of Coke when you do it this week. You can send that kind of stuff to that list. You can do the equivalent with email, right? You can drive traffic to a website purely based out of that demographic box, and you're going to be okay. If you're Sam's Club, maybe not quite, because now you need people to be willing to pay a membership fee in order to come into the store, you have a different proposition. Right. Therefore, maybe, maybe not. In some cases, it depends on how cheap you can reach, how broad that audience is. So if you're using email or you're down into say every door direct mail that the post office delivers, right, or Val pack. But now let's say you run a high end boutique of children's clothes and educational toys. Now Walmart sells children's clothes and Walmart sells some educational toys. But you have high end, pricier, more designer based stuff. Not only do you need a different income demographic, you probably need a different psycho and behavioral demographic, because we need parents who are either for the clothes in ego competition with other parents about how their children are dressed, or we need very concerned parents who care about having the best educational toys for their kids. So if we mail the same list, even if we were in the same price range, but we might not do as well as would Walmart. Got it. Got it. So let's talk about, let's give, we talked about the need to target market. We did a demographic information. Let's talk about psychic, psychographic information. Then I've actually coined a phrase which we'll get into which is psychic graphic information, which is based on a series of questions that you want people to answer about their target market. But what exactly is psychographic information? Well, so first of all, let me go through the list of all 17 things that you could use to do targeting. Okay. And then we'll come back and pick out the ones that match what you want. So their size of market, different businesses want different sizes of markets. How do we decide that? Well, what do you need to support yourself? Okay. Right. I mean, so if you want to build and support five dental offices with five docks in each one, you need a different size market that if you want to be a solo practitioner in an office, so a lot of it is what do you need to support yourself? Right. Reachability, can we reach them? Affordable reachability, can we reach them at a price we can afford? Okay. So modern rate, there's a modern railroad magazine for people who turn their basements into railroads. So if we sell stuff to modern railroaders, they're reachable. And if we can make the ad rates work, they're affordably reachable. They have an email list, they have a website we can advertise on, etc. Okay. There's no equivalent of that that I know of for people who might be susceptible to come to a magic show. Right. Right. So it's going to cost us more to go round them up. Right. Then to get them from somebody who's already around. So affordable reachability. Then three that go together, which kind of get to your psychographic issue is affinity, authority, celebrity, how well you match with the mindset of the market. There's points of leverage. Do you have a great testimonial that matches that market, etc. Your own customer facts, what do you know about the customers you got? Your best customer facts, who are your best customers, what do we know about them? Not just demographics, but psychographics, what do we know about them? And desired customer facts. So if you got customers right now and a bunch of them you don't like, and you want to use this to transition to customers you like, then you've got to have facts about them that are not just demographic, but psychographic. Then we have geography, maybe where they are. And then we have generic niche or subculture. Okay, explain that a little bit generic niche. So generic is like everybody. Okay, pulsing a wallet. Okay. Nice. Niche is they belong to an occupational, professional, vocational or business niche. So magicians is a niche. Dendis is a niche art. He's in the front architects front right architect somewhere. No, yes, architect. So architect niche. Okay. People with really bad annoying laughs. That's a subculture. That's not a subculture is a interest a hobby, a pursuit. So backyard gardeners is a subculture, not a niche. Harley Davidson owners is a subculture, not a niche. Economics ability to pay willingness to pay our prices or the prices at which we wish to sell. And then last how they buy. Do they buy in a way that we are able and willing to sell? Correct. So those are the 17 things. And those by the way are in the toolkit of those questions. So if you were trying to furiously write those down, they're inside the toolkit here. So let's delve a little, let's go deep into this, Dan. And let's talk about, let's talk about, let's go into level number three, which I call psychic graphic, because this is really getting inside their head. And you came up with a series of amazing questions, which were originally like the what keeps you up at night. Yeah, yeah, exactly. So let's let's go over some of those that we have here and explain why those are important. Well, so let's use that one. Okay, what keeps them up at night staring at the ceiling? What's the, let me just preface this is like such important information. Right. This is like key because this tells you how to modify the tools that the templates that are in here. So you're getting a perfect match to what's going on inside their head. Yeah, see message to market matches really about in B2B, the boardroom in consumer that at night kitchen table when people can't sleep kind of conversations and see different people buy the same thing for different reasons, which is why so I have a client right now who sells training on how to be an independent real estate investor. Okay. He happened his background, unlike most in that field, he does not have a I was living in a cardboard box under the bridge watching my dishes in the stream story. Okay. He actually had a really good career as an electrical engineer and made good mess. So one target market we are gearing up to market him to because he had generic marketing. Okay. Here, I can show you how to get rich rich in real estate. So it's like an infomercial. Come on, come on. Yeah, like somebody you would see late at night with the two babes in a hot tub, although I can't picture, but nonetheless. So, so now I'm making him a target marketer. So the first obvious place to go is other engineers. Okay, because back to our 17 point list, he has affinity with them. He already knows their tribal language. He can tell me their tribal language so I can help him. Okay, but an electric engineer with a good career who's making 60 or 100 grand a year has a set of motivations now that may cause him to be susceptible to this message. They are going to be a very different set of motivations than when we target dentists who are five to seven years away from retirement in practice. They have a totally different set of reasons why they might be interested in this message. Single moms or moms who wish to stay at home and want some way to supplement the income have a different set of reasons why they might be motivated by this message. Okay, so what's keeping them up at night? What's frustrating them? What's right? So the engineer is frustrated. He's worried about his career suddenly being pulled out from under him by a company moving from Colorado to South Carolina and his wife doesn't want to live in South Carolina. He really doesn't want to live in South Carolina or just wiping out the job all together. Right? The dentist does not have that worry. He owns his own practice. He's not going to fire himself. Right. So they're having different conversations with themselves. They see themselves differently. So when we market to the high school grad who has created a remodeling business, right? So the obvious reason for him to be interested in investing in real estate is because he knows how to fix stuff up, right? But that's logic, right? The kind of psychographic reason is at the Thanksgiving dinner every year, there's three sons in the family. One of them is a doctor. One of them is a lawyer. And there's him the disappointment. Okay? They all start with D's. But you know, so he is lawyer actually starts with an L. Yes, it does. Or attorney starts with an A. Yeah, but most lawyers start with these because they're damn lawyers to everybody else. So it's actually very good. So, so his psychographic reasons to be interested in this same thing, products the same, right? Message to market very different. Absolutely. And so again, there's a series of questions which are inside here, which you need to you need to answer as many as you possibly can before you actually start creating your marketing material. Dan, you tell a story and I'd like you to tell it because I most people probably haven't heard it about the when you were first getting started in this, you went out on someone who was selling carpet cleaning services, who totally had this thing. Oh, they make the mistake. Yeah, which is a really great illustration. So this is ancient history because you could get me on the phone, right? You still had to struggle. But so this is the I bought magnetic marketing system version either one or two is how old this was. I bought a marketing system and it doesn't work. Fine. What do you, you know, well, I took a mailing campaign out of it and I'm modified it for my carpet cleaning business and we mailed X number of pieces and we got no response. So who, where'd you mail it? Who'd you mail it to? So the person has an answer to the first the where question. He doesn't have any answer to the who because people don't think about that, right? I mailed it to all the homeowners. Like do a circle in my office. Yeah. So I picked a zip code. I, you know, I drew a circle around I always said they put the coffee cup upside down on a map. Now you do it on Google with a but still. So you put the coffee cup upside down, you draw a circle around it, depending upon your religious belief system of five mile radius, 10 mile radius, 15 mile radius of a business. And then there's my zip codes. And I'll buy Valpak there. I'll do direct mail there. I'll try and be at the top of the local search engines there. So I picked, he says, an area real close to the office. So it'd be easy for the guys in the trucks to take care of everybody, which made perfect sense to him. So now we do research, which is getting his truck and drive up and down the streets in these neighborhoods between 530 and 630 at night, which is when everybody comes back to their caves, but they don't shove the rock in the hole. So you get a look at them, right? And so these neighborhoods, this is Phoenix, are all identical small, cheap stucco houses on a concrete slab, pretty probably all built by the same builder down the street, real small lots, mostly consisting of dust, chain link fence kind of around everyone, all broken down, car up on blocks out in the driveway or in the front yard. Mama and Papa are out on the porch, the extension of the concrete slab, because they have an air conditioning in the house. So it really doesn't matter whether you're in or out, you know, they have what's called evaporative cooling, which is a long story, but trust me, if anybody ever tells you they have evaporative cooling and it's just like air conditioning, they are lying so badly you should shoot them. So they're out there for their happy hour, you know, cocktails before dinner. And they're in matching lawn chairs with the green and white plastic webbing, you know, you buy them at the CVS store and a couple straps are missing, you know, mama's hanging a little low, they're in matching undershirts, holes in them, he says hers or says his, or they both say I'm with stupid, you know, and the baby's out crawling around in the car parts and the dust in nothing but a diaper and there's a three legged one-eyed fleabit and mangy dog watching the kid and that's what you see, right, or worse. And so you don't have to, this research now can be brief because the question is, do these people look to you like the kind of folks who pay good money to have somebody come and clean their carpets, let alone on a regular or frequent basis? Well, of course not, right, if they clean them at all, because one answer is simply move furniture, but if they clean them at all once a year before the poor relatives come for the holidays, they go down to the Walmart store and they rent a machine and they buy a bottle of glock and they do it themselves, right, and so it doesn't matter what message you deliver to them, right, good, bad, persuasive, unpersuasive, doesn't matter how good a carpet cleaner you are, doesn't matter how many awards you've won, doesn't all that stuff that everybody thinks is important, doesn't matter that you've been certified by the Carpet Cleaning Institute of America, that you have a AAA rating from the Better Business Bureau, that you are third-generation business established in 1807 before there was soap, none of that matters because they don't pay to get their carpets clean, right, now that's a sort of an overly simplistic example of really what a lot of people do, they waste a lot of ammo that may be perfectly good ammo firing at targets that even if they hit them will be woefully unproductive, right, and then they say, well it doesn't work, so there's a, let's talk about this, there's a dirty little secret among copywriters and the dirty little secret is, well, the list selection, who you're mailing it to, you tell them, it's at least as important, if not more important than the message itself and the expertise invested in the craftsmanship of the message, so if you get this targeting really right, kind of a mediocre message will do the job, and therefore a mediocre copywriter can do just fine and candidly you might waste money if you climbed up the food chain to a better and better and better copywriter if you were hiring that, right, on the other hand if you get this really wrong, nobody's going to conquer that hill, right, they're just not going to conquer that hill, that, and so all this data mining and use of it is critically important and then the big leap is when it's available then a timing advantage on top of a data advantage, so for example in the hearing aid business, there's obviously an age demo, sweet spot, for hearing aids, slightly different one for females than for males, all right, and there's an income issue because they have to have enough money to buy them ability to pay, right, so we could be demographic in our approach to that, right, then there's some psychographic issues, all right, but now let's talk timing, so a male who fits our other profile is four times more likely to respond to marketing information about hearing aids between the third and sixth month after the death of his spouse than at any other time in his life, okay, four times more likely, understand, that's because he just lost his human hearing aid, all right, so when one person in a marriage is hearing is going bad worse or faster than the other, what's going on, right, well what did he say, what did he say, what did he say, all right, and so he has a human hearing aid, she died, right, for 90 days it doesn't matter, he goes into isolation, he grieves, he stays home, he doesn't socialize, if anything he sees only his immediate family and his closest buddy and they've already also adjusted, right, and been carrying him, right, but at about the three month point he starts to come out of his cave, not that he's down at the you know at the disco, but I mean he's you know, but I mean he's now at the disco, but now he's, at least you got it, at least you got it, I'm now entertaining myself, that's what I'm doing, yes folks, just this is the new updated version of the program, the bonus is we'll send you a written explanation of all the gags, the you know he's socializing on a wider basis, right, he's going back, he's playing in a poker game, he's going bowling, he's starting to write and he discovers the recognition sets in that he had a human hearing aid, the same kind of timing factor exists in all sorts of businesses, okay, birthdays affect all sorts of people, so in the skincare business if the marketing arrives during the week of a 50 to 68 year old woman's birthday, it'll be more effective than if the same marketing arrives to the same person at any other time during the year, which means if you want to use direct mail for example, we can take the list we would mail, we cut it into 12 based on birthday month, and we can spend all our money aiming at the ones who have higher susceptibility, so all of this allows us to use our money better because of this kind of matching. So one of the questions, so again the reason that we're this magnetic market, we just need to throw this together, all right, this is put together in a very specific format, so we went over the fundamentals in session number one, because you need to be grounded in the fundamentals, in this session we're going over how to target your markets, you got to follow these in the steps to get the results here, okay, so just don't jump and start creating marketing materials, do this first. So Dan, I guess the question that people would have is okay, how do I get this information? Well, so you start reverse engineering out of information you already have or could get from your own customers and clients. So what I say, I don't have any, do I survey them? Well first of all you now are more alert to it than you were before and you start to pay attention, okay, literally paying attention helps, absolutely, and most people don't, but beyond that you can survey, you can focus group, you can conversation, so you can do all, you can look into your own records because often that will tell you things. Businesses don't even really segment and pay attention to who's buying what from them, right, you know, so hardware store, one person is only buying things that have to do with paint and that's all they've ever bought and somebody else's, so hardly anybody even does that, so you can, you can mine out of your own data which also allows you to go reverse engineer into other data and into message, right, so this message to market match for example often will have, somebody will be there and they will have one website, right, which is crazy, well of course it's insane because even within their own customer base they have different people with different demographic, psychographic, different interests, different motivations for buying, right, and people that have big houses are different than people who have small houses, right, and so, so you can pull from yourself, right, you can, you can simply get it by self-select your lead generation, which I think is something you want to talk about later, but so you can, so you really can create self-selection by the way you generate leads, you can certainly go into the commercial marketplace of lists and you can privacy debtor by the day, so if you know what you want pretty much you can get it for a price and you can look at media based on who the reader is or the watcher, viewer, or listener and it's very important to do that not what the media is about so for example I have a client who is a wealth manager, only works with people who have recently sold or are in the process of selling a company for 10 million to 40 million dollars, so all he wants so a lead generation ad for him in Forbes is sort of obvious, fortune is sort of obvious we're having great success with a lead generation ad for him in a magazine called cowboys and indians and cowboys and indians is a magazine if people know what the rob report is it's sort of like the rob report for people who are interested in the west it's mostly a magazine of ads about high-priced ranch real estate, western themed home furnishings and real high-priced boots, clothes, apparel, jewelry and there's a lot of women's apparel and jewelry and stuff displayed on relatively has done with his money is gone and got the third wife who is one third his age who now he is spending on like there's no tomorrow so the fact that the editorial content is all about cowboys and indians it's irrelevant to us it's about who is the reader and by figuring that out by the way we get to show up alone because we're the only both manager ad there right right in Forbes there's 50 of demographic profile it has a very clear and known belief system right psychographic profile which means even if we're going to sell the same product or service and we're going to advertise on limbaugh more pieces in here too in the resource section answer all those questions as many as you can and then you're going to start developing your marketing message which is the next thing uh that we're little bit of information you're going to make the templates that are in here much more powerful and they're going to get you better results
